title: Cambridge Challenge
seoDescription: Take a fast Cambridge-style reasoning quiz with staged visual logic, number patterns, deduction, and analytical puzzles.
pageTitle: Only 4% Can Pass This Cambridge Test
summary: A staged 80-question challenge inspired by the reasoning, clarity, and problem-solving expected in high-level university entrance tests.

homepage.title: Can You Pass This Cambridge Entrance Test?
homepage.summary: Test your logic, evidence reading, number sense, and academic reasoning across 10 increasingly difficult stages.

infoPanel.title: About This Test
infoPanel.intro: This quiz is an entertainment and self-challenge test inspired by the reasoning skills often valued in selective university admissions. It is not an official Cambridge University admissions test.

columns.title: How It Works
columns.body: You will move through 10 stages of 8 questions. The early rounds test clear thinking and core skills, while later rounds require sharper analysis, careful comparison, and multi-step reasoning.

columns.title: About This Topic
columns.body: The quiz covers academic-style reasoning across logic, language, numbers, evidence, science, ethics, and problem solving, with each stage built around a different sub-theme.

infoPanel.footerBody: Every question has one correct answer and a short explanation. Scores are for entertainment and self-challenge only.

landing.challengeText: The final rounds are designed to test calm, precise reasoning.

profiles.title: You handled the hardest reasoning.
profiles.copy: Exceptional performance across the full challenge. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: A strong academic result.
profiles.copy: You stayed accurate through demanding stages. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: A solid, thoughtful score.
profiles.copy: You showed good reasoning across several stages. Your strongest area was {stage}.

profiles.title: Keep sharpening the method.
profiles.copy: The hardest questions reward careful reading and patient logic. Your best area was {stage}.

stageGroups.title: Academic Warm-Up

questions.prompt: A tutor says a strong answer should be clear, justified, and relevant. Which response best follows that advice?
choices: It gives a direct answer and explains the reason
It uses the longest possible vocabulary
It repeats the question in different words
It avoids taking a clear position
questions.explanation: A strong academic answer should state a position and support it with a reason.

questions.prompt: Which skill is most useful when deciding whether a source supports a conclusion?
choices: Remembering the title of the source
Checking what the source actually says
Counting how many paragraphs it has
Looking only at the final sentence
questions.explanation: Evidence must be judged by its actual content, not by surface features.

questions.prompt: A sequence increases by 3 each time: 4, 7, 10, 13. What comes next?
questions.explanation: The pattern adds 3 each time, so 13 becomes 16.

questions.prompt: Which statement is an opinion rather than a fact?
choices: Water freezes at 0 degrees Celsius under standard conditions
This essay is more interesting than the other one
questions.explanation: Interest is a judgement, so it depends on personal opinion.

questions.prompt: If every member of Group A is in Group B, and Mira is in Group A, what must be true?
choices: Group B has only one member
Group A and Group B are the same size
questions.explanation: If all members of A are inside B, any member of A must also be in B.

questions.prompt: Which pair shows the strongest contrast?
questions.explanation: Temporary and permanent are opposites, while the other pairs are similar in meaning.

questions.prompt: A student has 12 books and gives away one quarter of them. How many books remain?
questions.explanation: One quarter of 12 is 3, so 9 books remain.

questions.prompt: Which question is most useful for checking a claim?
choices: Is the claim written in a large font?
Is the claim short enough to remember?
Who benefits if this claim is accepted?
questions.explanation: Asking who benefits can reveal possible bias or motivation.

stageGroups.encouragement: Good start. The next stage asks you to read more carefully and separate evidence from impression.

stageGroups.title: Clear Reading

questions.prompt: A passage says, 'The experiment reduced errors, but it did not remove them.' Which summary is most accurate?
choices: Errors became less frequent but still existed
questions.explanation: The phrase says errors decreased, not that they disappeared.

questions.prompt: Which word best completes the relationship: evidence is to conclusion as foundation is to what?
questions.explanation: Evidence supports a conclusion as a foundation supports a building.

questions.prompt: A paragraph gives three reasons for a claim. What should you check first?
choices: Whether the paragraph uses short sentences
Whether the claim appears at the top
Whether the reasons are relevant to the claim
questions.explanation: Relevant reasons are central to whether the argument works.

questions.prompt: Which statement is the most cautious academic claim?
choices: This may suggest a possible explanation
questions.explanation: A cautious claim recognises limits and avoids overstating certainty.

questions.prompt: A text says a policy 'may increase participation'. What can safely be inferred?
choices: An increase is possible but not guaranteed
The policy is impossible to measure
questions.explanation: The word 'may' signals possibility, not certainty.

questions.prompt: Which sentence gives the clearest cause-and-effect relationship?
choices: The room was bright, and the chairs were blue
Because the road was icy, the bus moved slowly
The lecture was long and held on Tuesday
questions.explanation: It clearly links icy roads as the cause of slow movement.

questions.prompt: If a writer uses one example to represent a whole group, what should you question?
choices: Whether the example has a name
Whether the group is mentioned first
Whether one example is enough evidence
Whether the sentence is too short
questions.explanation: A single example may not fairly represent a wider group.

questions.prompt: A text compares two plans but gives costs for only one. What is the main weakness?
choices: The cheaper plan must be better
questions.explanation: A fair comparison needs relevant information for both options.

stageGroups.encouragement: Strong reading. Now the numbers start to matter more.

stageGroups.title: Number Sense

questions.prompt: A value rises from 50 to 60. What is the percentage increase?
questions.explanation: The increase is 10, which is 20% of 50.

questions.prompt: Which number completes the sequence: 2, 4, 8, 16, ?
questions.explanation: Each term is doubled, so 16 becomes 32.

questions.prompt: A class has 18 students. Two thirds choose biology. How many choose biology?
questions.explanation: Two thirds of 18 is 12.

questions.prompt: If 5 notebooks cost the same total as 2 folders, and one folder costs 10 units, what does one notebook cost?
questions.explanation: Two folders cost 20 units, so each of 5 notebooks costs 4 units.

questions.prompt: A graph line goes steadily downward from left to right. What does this usually show?
choices: A decrease over time or across the measured range
questions.explanation: A downward line usually represents a falling value.

questions.prompt: A sample of 100 people includes 55 who prefer option A. What fraction prefer option A?
questions.explanation: 55 out of 100 simplifies to 11 out of 20.

questions.prompt: Which average is most affected by one unusually large value?
questions.explanation: The mean uses every value, so an extreme value can pull it upward.

questions.prompt: A ratio of students to tutors is 12:3. What is the simplified ratio?
questions.explanation: Both parts divide by 3, giving 4:1.

stageGroups.encouragement: You have cleared the first quantitative stage. Next, look for hidden assumptions.

stageGroups.title: Evidence and Assumptions

questions.prompt: A study finds that students who sleep more score higher. What extra evidence is needed before saying sleep caused the higher scores?
choices: Whether the scores were written in a table
Whether the study title was clear
questions.explanation: Correlation alone does not prove cause, so other factors matter.

questions.prompt: Which statement is an assumption?
choices: Higher attendance always means better learning
questions.explanation: It makes a broad claim that would need support.

questions.prompt: A conclusion is based on data from 12 people. What is the best concern?
choices: The sample may be too small
questions.explanation: A small sample may not represent a wider population well.

questions.prompt: Which evidence would best support the claim that a course improved writing skills?
choices: Students wrote longer essays after the course
The course had an attractive website
Independent markers rated post-course essays higher using the same criteria
questions.explanation: Independent ratings using the same criteria directly assess improvement.

questions.prompt: A researcher changes two variables at the same time. What problem does this create?
choices: It may be unclear which variable caused the result
The experiment becomes impossible to record
questions.explanation: Changing two variables makes cause harder to identify.

questions.prompt: Which question best tests whether a survey is biased?
choices: Was the survey printed on paper?
Were the questions leading or neutral?
Did the survey have a title?
Were the answers placed in boxes?
questions.explanation: Leading questions can push respondents toward certain answers.

questions.prompt: An argument says, 'This idea is popular, so it must be correct.' What is the weakness?
choices: The argument is too short to read
questions.explanation: Many people believing something does not make it true.

questions.prompt: Which source is likely to be most reliable for a scientific claim?
choices: A peer-reviewed study with clear methods
questions.explanation: Clear methods and peer review make a scientific source easier to assess.

stageGroups.encouragement: Your reasoning is warming up. The next stage tests scientific thinking rather than memorised facts.

stageGroups.title: Scientific Reasoning

questions.prompt: In an experiment, the control group is used mainly to do what?
choices: Provide a comparison for the tested condition
Choose the final answer in advance
questions.explanation: A control group helps show what changes when the tested condition is introduced.

questions.prompt: Which claim is best supported by repeated similar results from different studies?
questions.explanation: Replication across studies usually strengthens confidence in a finding.

questions.prompt: A plant receives less light and grows more slowly. Which variable was changed?
questions.explanation: Light exposure is the condition deliberately changed.

questions.prompt: Which statement is a hypothesis?
choices: The beaker is made of glass
If temperature rises, the reaction may happen faster
questions.explanation: A hypothesis is a testable proposed explanation or prediction.

questions.prompt: A measurement is precise but not accurate. What does that mean?
choices: It gives similar results that are not close to the true value
It is close to the true value every time
questions.explanation: Precision means consistency; accuracy means closeness to the true value.

questions.prompt: Which observation best suggests a chemical reaction has occurred?
choices: A new gas forms during mixing
A book is placed on a table
questions.explanation: Gas formation can be evidence of a chemical reaction.

questions.prompt: A model is useful in science mainly because it can do what?
choices: Represent a complex system in a simpler form
questions.explanation: Models simplify complex systems so they can be studied and tested.

questions.prompt: Which result would most challenge a proposed theory?
choices: A result predicted by the theory
A result unrelated to the theory
A result shown in a neat table
A repeatable result that contradicts the theory
questions.explanation: A repeatable contradiction is strong evidence against a theory.

stageGroups.encouragement: Stage 5 complete. Now the questions move into social evidence and historical judgement.

stageGroups.title: Historical and Social Judgement

questions.prompt: A historian compares two accounts of the same event. What should they check first?
choices: Who wrote each account and why
Which account has a nicer cover
questions.explanation: Author perspective and purpose affect how evidence should be interpreted.

questions.prompt: Which evidence is strongest for showing a change over time?
choices: One description from a single year
Several comparable records from different years
A modern opinion about the past
questions.explanation: Comparable records across years can reveal a pattern of change.

questions.prompt: A map shows trade routes between cities. What can it most directly help explain?
choices: How goods and ideas may have moved
Which language was easiest to learn
questions.explanation: Trade routes can show possible movement of goods, people, and ideas.

questions.prompt: A social policy benefits one group but disadvantages another. What academic skill is most needed to assess it fairly?
choices: Assuming the policy must be good
questions.explanation: Fair assessment requires weighing effects across more than one perspective.

questions.prompt: Which statement best separates description from explanation?
choices: Description says what happened; explanation says why it happened
Description is always false; explanation is always true
Description uses numbers; explanation never does
questions.explanation: Description identifies events or features; explanation gives reasons or causes.

questions.prompt: Why might a primary source still be unreliable?
choices: It is always written too late
It may reflect bias, limited knowledge, or a specific purpose
It must agree with every other source
questions.explanation: Being close to an event does not remove bias or limitation.

questions.prompt: A claim says one invention alone caused a major social change. What is the best objection?
choices: Major social changes often have several causes
questions.explanation: Large social changes are usually influenced by multiple factors.

questions.prompt: Which comparison is fairest when studying two countries?
choices: Comparing one city with an entire continent
Using one statistic for one country and a different statistic for the other
Choosing only the most surprising example
Comparing similar periods and similar measures
questions.explanation: Fair comparison needs similar time periods and comparable measures.

stageGroups.encouragement: You are past the midpoint. Next comes ethical reasoning with carefully balanced choices.

stageGroups.title: Philosophy and Ethics

questions.prompt: In ethical reasoning, what is a trade-off?
choices: A situation where gaining one value may reduce another
A calculation with no human effect
A rule that answers every case automatically
A decision with only one possible result
questions.explanation: A trade-off occurs when values or benefits conflict.

questions.prompt: Which question best tests whether a rule is applied fairly?
choices: Would the same rule apply to similar cases?
Is the rule easy to memorise?
questions.explanation: Fairness often requires treating relevantly similar cases consistently.

questions.prompt: A decision helps many people but harms a few. Which issue is most directly raised?
choices: The speed of writing the decision
The balance between overall benefit and individual cost
questions.explanation: The case raises a common ethical tension between collective benefit and individual harm.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes a principle?
choices: A general standard used to guide judgement
questions.explanation: A principle is a general guide for evaluating choices.

questions.prompt: Two people disagree because they value freedom and safety differently. What is the deepest source of disagreement?
choices: They give different weight to competing values
They cannot read the same facts
One of them must be dishonest
questions.explanation: They may accept the same facts but prioritise values differently.

questions.prompt: Which argument is strongest?
choices: This action is right because I prefer it
This action is right because it follows a clear principle and avoids serious harm
This action is right because it is traditional
This action is right because it is easy
questions.explanation: It gives both a principle and a consequence-based reason.

questions.prompt: A policy treats everyone identically but ignores major differences in need. What concern does this raise?
choices: Equal treatment may not always produce fair outcomes
questions.explanation: Sometimes fairness requires attention to relevant differences.

questions.prompt: Which question best reveals a hidden ethical assumption?
choices: How many letters are in the policy title?
Is the speaker using a calm voice?
Does the example appear first or last?
What value is being treated as most important?
questions.explanation: Identifying the priority value helps reveal the assumption behind the judgement.

stageGroups.encouragement: Good progress. The next stage is more abstract and pattern-heavy.

stageGroups.title: Abstract Patterns

questions.prompt: Which item breaks the pattern: square, triangle, circle, rectangle?
questions.explanation: Circle is the only shape listed without straight sides.

questions.prompt: Complete the analogy: seed is to plant as idea is to what?
questions.explanation: A seed can develop into a plant; an idea can develop into an argument.

questions.prompt: Which sequence follows a pattern of adding increasing odd numbers: 1, 4, 9, 16, ?
questions.explanation: These are square numbers: 1, 2 squared, 3 squared, 4 squared, then 5 squared.

questions.prompt: In the set {2, 3, 5, 8, 13}, which number does not share the same property as the others if the property is prime?
questions.explanation: 8 is not prime; the other listed numbers are prime.

questions.prompt: A pattern alternates between adding 2 and multiplying by 2: 3, 5, 10, 12, 24. What comes next?
questions.explanation: After multiplying by 2 to get 24, the next step is adding 2.

questions.prompt: Which pair has the same relationship as author and book?
questions.explanation: An author creates a book as a painter creates a painting.

questions.prompt: If all blue tokens are heavy and no heavy tokens are small, what must be true about blue tokens?
questions.explanation: Blue tokens are heavy, and heavy tokens are not small.

questions.prompt: A table lists A above B, B above C, and C above D. Which item is lowest?
questions.explanation: The order places D below C, so D is lowest.

stageGroups.encouragement: You handled the abstract round. The next stage combines several skills at once.

stageGroups.title: Multi-Step Problems

questions.prompt: A train travels 180 kilometres in 3 hours. If it keeps the same speed, how far will it travel in 5 hours?
questions.explanation: The speed is 60 kilometres per hour, so in 5 hours it travels 300 kilometres.

questions.prompt: A claim has three premises. Two are true, but one is irrelevant. What happens to the argument?
choices: It may be weakened because relevance matters
questions.explanation: True premises still need to support the conclusion.

questions.prompt: A committee must choose the option with the lowest cost among those that meet the safety standard. Option A costs 80 and fails safety. Option B costs 95 and meets safety. Option C costs 90 and meets safety. Which should it choose?
questions.explanation: Only B and C meet safety, and C has the lower cost among them.

questions.prompt: A box contains 3 red, 2 blue, and 5 green tokens. What is the probability of choosing a blue token?
questions.explanation: There are 2 blue tokens out of 10 total, which is 1/5.

questions.prompt: A report says test scores rose after a new timetable, but also says extra tutoring began at the same time. What is the main issue?
choices: There is a possible confounding factor
The timetable is the only possible cause
questions.explanation: Extra tutoring could also explain the score increase.

questions.prompt: Four applicants are ranked by interview and written score. The rule says the written score breaks ties. Lina and Omar have equal interviews; Lina has a higher written score. Who ranks higher?
questions.explanation: The written score breaks the interview tie, so Lina ranks higher.

questions.prompt: A machine doubles a number and then subtracts 5. If the output is 17, what was the input?
questions.explanation: Solving 2x minus 5 equals 17 gives x equals 11.

questions.prompt: Which conclusion follows: Some researchers are mathematicians. All mathematicians use logic. Therefore...
questions.explanation: The researchers who are mathematicians use logic, so some researchers use logic.

stageGroups.encouragement: One stage remains. The final round rewards calm, precise academic judgement.

questions.prompt: A passage argues that a policy should be adopted because it is efficient, but it ignores whether it is fair. What is the strongest critique?
choices: The argument leaves out a relevant ethical dimension
Efficiency is the only academic value
questions.explanation: A full evaluation may need both efficiency and fairness.

questions.prompt: A dataset has values 4, 4, 5, 7, 30. Which measure best represents the typical value if you want to reduce the effect of the outlier?
questions.explanation: The median is less affected by the unusually high value 30.

questions.prompt: A scientist predicts that increasing pressure will reduce volume. The results are: pressure rises, volume falls in every trial. What is the best description?
choices: The results are unrelated to the prediction
questions.explanation: The observed pattern matches the prediction.

questions.prompt: Which assumption is needed for this argument: 'The library should extend hours because more students asked for evening access.'
choices: Evening access would help address the request
questions.explanation: The argument assumes longer hours would meet the demand for evening access.

questions.prompt: A pattern uses two rules: multiply by 3, then subtract 2. Starting at 2 gives 6, then 4, then 12, then 10. What comes next?
questions.explanation: After subtracting 2 to get 10, the next rule is multiply by 3.

questions.prompt: In a debate, one speaker attacks the person making an argument instead of the argument itself. What is the main flaw?
choices: It changes the subject from evidence to personal criticism
It proves the opponent is correct
questions.explanation: Personal attacks do not address whether the argument is sound.

questions.prompt: A university interview question asks why a conclusion might be true, not whether it is popular. What skill is being tested?
questions.explanation: The question asks for reasons that support a conclusion.

questions.prompt: A fair test compares two methods. Method A is tested on beginners, and Method B is tested on experts. What is the problem?
choices: The groups differ in a relevant way before the test
questions.explanation: Different starting ability makes the comparison unfair.
